page.content: The following table presents a comprehensive overview of re-interventions conducted on patients over an eight-year period from 2001 to 2008, highlighting trends in the frequency of these interventions categorized by life stage.

page.content: Specifically, the data reveals that the total number of re-interventions in the same patient decreased from 3,268 in 2001 to 3,039 in 2008, indicating a downward trend in the necessity for repeat procedures. This decline prompts further examination of the underlying factors contributing to this phenomenon, particularly in the context of patient outcomes and treatment efficacy across different life stages, such as Seed, Seedling, Plantlet, and Juvenile. Additionally, the Age-Adjusted Percentage Change (AAPC) with a confidence interval of 95% indicates a significant shift in re-intervention patterns, underscoring the importance of longitudinal data in assessing the effectiveness of medical interventions over time. Understanding these trends not only aids in improving patient management strategies but also provides insight into the efficacy of initial treatments and the overall healthcare delivery system. The implications of these findings will be critical for healthcare practitioners and policymakers aiming to enhance patient care and optimize resource allocation in clinical settings.
